q: A colleague says their canvas app logic 'defines each step to reach the goal, trading simplicity for fine-grained control.' Which development style is that?
why: Imperative development focuses on HOW: you define each step and gain control at the cost of complexity. Declarative focuses on WHAT result you want.

q: You must store the signed-in user's DisplayName once at launch and read it from any screen. Which function creates that value?
why: Set creates a GLOBAL variable readable anywhere in the app. UpdateContext is screen-scoped; Collect/Patch write to data sources/collections.

q: Decide whether each statement about Power Apps variables is true.

rows.s: A context variable is available only on the screen where UpdateContext created it.
UpdateContext can declare several context variables in a single call.
The Set function can declare multiple global variables in one call, separated by commas.
Global variables are generally the recommended default when in doubt.

why: UpdateContext can set many variables at once and is screen-scoped; Set defines one global variable per call, and globals are the flexible default.

q: Which of the following are genuine Power Apps variable mechanisms? (choose 2)
why: Power Apps uses global variables (Set), context variables (UpdateContext) and collections. Environment variables belong to solutions/Dataverse, and there are no typed module constants in Power Fx.

q: The same expensive CountRows(Filter(...)) drives the Visible of both an icon and a button. What is the more performant, maintainable fix?
o: Run it once in the screen OnVisible, store the boolean in a variable, and bind both controls to that variable
Duplicate the formula on each control's Visible property
Move the formula into the app OnStart only
Wrap each formula in a separate ForAll
why: Computing once into a variable avoids duplicate network/app/data-source work and keeps the logic in one place.

q: You copy controls that use UpdateContext({varShowPopUp:true}) from Screen1 to Screen2. What happens to varShowPopUp?
o: Each screen keeps its own independent value under the same name
Both screens now share one synchronized value
The paste fails because context names must be unique
The variable is promoted to a global automatically
why: Context variables are scoped per screen, so identically named ones on different screens hold independent values.

q: For reading the user's name repeatedly, which option is the MOST performant?
o: A global variable set once in OnStart
A context variable set on each screen's OnVisible
Calling User() inline wherever it's needed
Recomputing it in a collection per screen
why: A global set once in OnStart avoids re-hitting the data source; a context variable still queries each time the page opens, and inline User() is the slowest.

q: Decide whether each statement is true when a variable stores a boolean like varOutstandingExceeded.

rows.s: A control's Visible property accepts the boolean variable directly, needing no extra formula.
Power Apps infers the variable's type (Boolean) from the value you assign.
You must explicitly declare the variable's data type before using it.

why: Power Apps types variables implicitly from the assigned value; a boolean variable binds straight to Visible.

q: Set(varCounter, varCounter + 1) sits on a button's OnSelect. Before it is ever set, what is varCounter's starting value?
why: An uninitialized number variable defaults to 0, so the first click makes it 1.

q: After Set(varUser, User()), how do you show the user's email in a label?
why: A variable can hold a whole record; you read its columns with dot notation, e.g. varUser.Email.

q: A variable counts invoices via OnStart. A user adds an invoice but the count doesn't change. Why?
o: Variables don't auto-update; the value only changes if you recompute it or the app restarts
OnStart runs continuously and should refresh it
why: Variables are not reactive to data-source changes; you must update them explicitly or on the next OnStart.

q: Decide the default value of each uninitialized variable type.

rows.s: A text variable defaults to an empty string "".
A boolean variable defaults to true.

why: Defaults are "" for text, 0 for number and false for boolean.
